title: Percentage of Americans who live alone, by age
subtitle: An individual is defined as living alone when there is one person in the household and does not live in group quarters.
caption:
Percentage of Americans who live alone, by age

An individual is defined as living alone when there is one person in the household and does not live in group quarters.

This line chart tracks the share of the United States population living alone across six specific age cohorts from 1900 to 2018. The vertical axis measures the percentage of people within each age group living alone, ranging from 0% to 45%, while the horizontal axis marks time in twenty-year intervals. The data series are distinguished by color and labeled by age: Age 89 (dark blue), Age 75 (maroon), Age 60 (purple), Age 30 (brown), Age 45 (green), Age 21 (orange), and Age 18 (light blue). In 1900, the highest rate of solo living occurred among 75-year-olds at roughly 6%, while the 89-year-old cohort started lower at approximately 3%. Over the following century, the trajectory for the oldest Americans shifted significantly; the line for Age 89 rises steadily, crossing the Age 75 line around 1975 and reaching a peak of roughly 42% by 2018. The Age 75 group shows a different pattern, climbing to a high of roughly 32% in 1980 before declining to roughly 25% in 2018. Middle-aged groups show moderate increases; the Age 60 line rises from under 5% in 1900 to roughly 18% in 2018. The lines for Age 30 and Age 45 remain low until 1960, then rise to cluster together around the 10% mark by 2018. The youngest cohorts, Age 21 and Age 18, stay near the bottom of the chart throughout the entire period, with values consistently below 5%. The visual arrangement shows a clear divergence where the likelihood of living alone increases with age, particularly for those over 75 in the modern era.
